q: In a circular motion, the object just moves in a circle.[cite: 1]
A rigid body is a body that continuously deforms or changes shape.[cite: 1]
No matter how the body moves, the distance between any two particles within a rigid body remains constant.[cite: 1]
If a rigid body is in rotational motion, all the particles constituting it undergo circular motion about a common axis.[cite: 1]
During time intervals when an object speeds up or slows down, its angular velocity remains constant.[cite: 1]
If its angular velocity changes at a constant rate, then the angular acceleration is constant.[cite: 1]
The equations for constant angular acceleration in rotational motion are completely different from those for linear motion.[cite: 1]
The equations for constant angular acceleration in rotational motion are analogous to equations for constant acceleration in linear motion.[cite: 1]
All particles of an object rotating around the axis of rotation move in a circular path.[cite: 1]
The relation between arc length $s$ and angular displacement $\theta$ is measured in degrees.[cite: 1]
The instantaneous angular velocity of rotational object is the same for all the particles comprising the object.[cite: 1]
The linear velocity is also called tangential velocity because its direction is tangent to the path.[cite: 1]
If the magnitude of the angular velocity of a rotating object changes, the magnitude of linear velocity will remain constant.[cite: 1]
The direction of tangential acceleration is always perpendicular to the circular path.[cite: 1]
The tangential acceleration of a rotating object is a measure of how fast the tangential velocity changes.[cite: 1]
The direction of the angular velocity vector is parallel to the plane of rotation.[cite: 1]
The direction of the angular velocity vector is perpendicular to the plane of rotation, and along the axis of rotation.[cite: 1]
When the angular velocity is decreasing, the angular acceleration vector points in the opposite to the angular velocity.[cite: 1]
An object that moves in a circle at constant speed $v$ is said to experience uniform circular motion.[cite: 1]
In uniform circular motion, the direction of the velocity remains constant.[cite: 1]
The change in direction of velocity constitutes an acceleration.[cite: 1]
An object revolving in a circle is continuously accelerating, even when the speed remains constant.[cite: 1]
Centripetal acceleration is denoted by $\vec{a}_c$ and is directed along the radius towards the centre of the circle.[cite: 1]
The centripetal acceleration and linear velocity are parallel to each other at every point in the path.[cite: 1]
In nonuniform circular motion, an object is moving in a circular path with a constant speed.[cite: 1]
Unlike tangential acceleration, centripetal acceleration is present in both uniform and nonuniform circular motion.[cite: 1]
An object in nonuniform circular motion has a resultant acceleration that is the scalar sum of the centripetal and tangential accelerations.[cite: 1]
The two acceleration vectors $\vec{a}_c$ and $\vec{a}_T$ are perpendicular to each other.[cite: 1]
